🏗️ System Architecture

What is this diagram?
This flowchart shows how all components of the DreamCoder card game modeling system fit together. Data flows from bottom to top: starting with the core domain definitions, through processing and analysis, up to the DreamCoder components (some still to be implemented), and finally to this report output.
Figure 0: System Architecture Diagram
Green boxes are complete components (including the trained recognition network at 93.5% accuracy). Blue boxes handle data processing. Purple boxes perform analysis. Red boxes are DreamCoder components still to be implemented. Orange is the output layer.

🔑 Component Descriptions

  • cards.py: Defines Card, Hand, Suit, Rank, Color, Parity types. The foundation.
  • primitives.py: 60+ compositional functions organized in 5 levels (0-4).
  • catalogue.py: All 57 rules with their compositional decompositions.
  • Task Generation: Samples random hands, evaluates rules, creates training examples.
  • Feature Extraction: Converts example hands into 158-dimensional numeric vectors.
  • Primitive/Subtree/Abstraction Analysis: Identifies shared structure across rules.
  • Recognition Network: ✅ COMPLETE! Neural network achieving 93.47% accuracy.
  • Enumeration Search: Searches program space for rules matching examples (to implement).
  • Library Learning: Discovers new abstractions from solved tasks (to implement).
  • Wake-Sleep Loop: Alternates solving tasks and learning, improving over iterations.

📊 Overview & Summary Statistics

What is this report?
This report documents the complete compositional analysis of 57 card game rules. Each rule is decomposed into primitive functions following the DreamCoder framework (Ellis et al., 2023). The goal is to understand shared structure across rules that may explain transfer learning in human participants.
57
Total Rules
16
Rule Families
9
Discovered Abstractions
60
Unique Primitives

Rules by Family

Family Count Description Example Rule
ADJ 3 Adjacent/skip constraints Adj_same_rank_or_suit
ALTCLR 3 Alternative color groupings AltColor1_palindrome
AP 3 Arithmetic progression detection AP_len3_anywhere_anyk
CENTER 2 Distance from center rules Halves_radial_nonincreasing
COPY 6 Halves copy sequence patterns Halves_copy_suits
COUNT 6 Counting and set cardinality rules Has_pair_ranks
HIER 6 Hierarchical (halves share boolean property) Halves_uniform_color_equal
LANG 3 Language/grammar rules (bracket matching) Well_formed_brackets_by_suit
LOCAL 4 Simple positional or ordering constraints Sorted_by_rank
MAP 6 Suit cycle mapping rules Half_map_samepos_M1
PAL 3 Palindrome patterns Suits_palindrome
PARITY 2 Odd/even rank rules Only_one_odd_rank
POSITION 2 Specific position constraints Pos3_is_JQK
SCORE 3 Scoring formulas Score_threshold_Rstar
SHIFT 3 Positional rank differences Shift_half_plus_two
TOKEN 2 Specific card presence Has_Ace_of_Spades

🔄 How the Model Works: A Typical Run

What happens when we run the model?
This section explains step-by-step what the DreamCoder-style model does when processing our card game rules. Understanding this pipeline is essential for interpreting the results.

Step 1: Task Generation

For each rule, we generate training examples:
  1. Sample N random 6-card hands (e.g., N=100)
  2. Evaluate the rule on each hand → TRUE or FALSE
  3. Result: A "task" = list of (hand, label) pairs
Example for "Sorted_by_rank":
[2♠, 5♦, 7♣, 9♥, J♦, K♠] → TRUE (ranks increase)
[K♠, 2♦, 7♣, 5♥, 9♦, J♠] → FALSE (not sorted)

Step 2: Feature Extraction

Each task is converted to a 104-dimensional feature vector:
  • Rank statistics (8 dims): mean, std, min, max for positive/negative examples
  • Suit entropy (2 dims): how evenly distributed are suits?
  • Color uniformity (2 dims): are examples all one color?
  • Structural features (20+ dims): has pair, is sorted, is palindrome, etc.
  • Positional features (40+ dims): what's at each position?
  • Relational features (30+ dims): terminal equality, halves similarity, etc.
These features help the recognition network identify which primitives are relevant.

Step 3: Recognition Network (Neural Guidance)

✅ TRAINED AND WORKING! The recognition network is now fully implemented:
  • Input: 158-dimensional feature vector (21 per card × 6 + 30 global + 2 label)
  • Architecture: Attention-based aggregation → FC layers → 60 primitive outputs
  • Output: Multi-hot probability for each of 60 primitives
  • Training: 5,700 tasks, 30 epochs, BCEWithLogitsLoss
  • Final Accuracy: 93.47%
This network guides program search by predicting which primitives are relevant for each task!

Step 4: Program Enumeration (Search)

⚠️ Not yet implemented — This is where the model searches for programs:
  • Goal: Find a program (composition of primitives) that matches all examples
  • Method: Best-first search, prioritized by recognition network scores
  • Output: The simplest program that explains the examples
Example: Given examples for "Sorted_by_rank", find: λh. is_sorted(map(get_rank_val, h))

Step 5: Library Learning (Compression)

⚠️ Not yet implemented — After solving many tasks, we learn abstractions:
  • Goal: Find reusable "chunks" that appear in multiple rules
  • Method: Identify common subtrees in solved programs
  • Output: New library entries (e.g., halves_equal, seq_palindrome)
The "Discovered Abstractions" section shows what we expect DreamCoder to find.

Understanding Accuracy Metrics

✅ REAL Recognition Network Metrics (Trained Model)

The accuracy values shown are from a trained recognition network achieving 93.47% overall accuracy on primitive prediction:
  • Training Data: 5,700 tasks (100 per rule × 57 rules)
  • Architecture: 158-dim features → attention aggregation → 60-output multilabel classifier
  • Training: 30 epochs, Adam optimizer, BCEWithLogitsLoss
  • Validation: 20% held-out per rule
What the accuracy means:
  • Per-rule accuracy: % of primitives correctly predicted for that rule's tasks
  • Range: 83.3% (Score_threshold_Rstar) to 99.3% (Sorted_by_rank)
These are real model predictions, not simulations!

📈 Visualizations & Analysis

How to read these visualizations:
Each figure below reveals different aspects of the compositional structure of our rule set. Hover over elements or refer to the legends for detailed explanations.
Figure 1: Distribution of Rules by Family This bar chart shows how many rules belong to each family. Families are conceptual groupings based on the type of constraint (e.g., counting, position, palindrome). Larger families indicate common computational patterns in our rule set.

📖 Legend for Figure 1: Distribution of Rules by Family

  • Height of bar = number of rules in that family
  • Colors are for visual distinction only
  • Families with more rules may have more transfer potential
Figure 2: Rules by Compositional Complexity Level Rules are classified by their maximum compositional level (0-4). Higher levels indicate more complex compositions. Level 0 uses only atomic primitives; Level 4 uses meta-combinators like halves_equal or seq_palindrome.

📖 Legend for Figure 2: Rules by Compositional Complexity Level

  • Level 0 (Atomic): Simple property checks (e.g., get_suit, at)
  • Level 1 (Combinators): List operations (e.g., map, filter, count)
  • Level 2 (Structural): Hand decomposition (e.g., halves, shifted_pairs)
  • Level 3 (Domain): Specialized algorithms (e.g., hasAP, bracket_match)
  • Level 4 (Meta): Higher-order patterns (e.g., halves_equal, seq_palindrome)
Figure 3: Model Accuracy Heatmap (Real - Trained Recognition Network) This heatmap shows the ACTUAL accuracy of the trained recognition network (93.47% overall) on each rule. Green = high accuracy, Red = low accuracy. These are real results from training.

📖 Legend for Figure 3: Model Accuracy Heatmap (Real - Trained Recognition Network)

  • Color scale: 0.5 (red/chance) to 1.0 (green/perfect)
  • Each cell represents one rule
  • Overall accuracy: 93.47% (real training result)
Figure 4: Rule Difficulty vs Base Rate Scatter plot showing estimated difficulty against base rate (how often random hands satisfy the rule). Rules with extreme base rates (near 0 or 1) are easier because guessing works well. Rules near 0.5 base rate are hardest.

📖 Legend for Figure 4: Rule Difficulty vs Base Rate

  • X-axis: Base rate (0=never satisfied, 1=always satisfied)
  • Y-axis: Estimated difficulty (0=easy, 1=hard)
  • Each point is one rule, colored by family
  • Dashed lines mark 0.5 thresholds
Figure 5: Discovered Abstractions by Frequency Bar chart showing how many rules use each discovered abstraction. These are compositional patterns that appear in multiple rules - they represent reusable "chunks" that may transfer between related rules.

📖 Legend for Figure 5: Discovered Abstractions by Frequency

  • Bar length = number of rules using this abstraction
  • Higher frequency = more potential for transfer learning
  • These abstractions are candidates for DreamCoder's library learning
Figure 6: Primitive Co-occurrence Matrix Heatmap showing how often pairs of primitives appear together in rules. Bright colors indicate frequent co-occurrence. This reveals which primitives form natural "bundles" that might be abstracted.

📖 Legend for Figure 6: Primitive Co-occurrence Matrix

  • Diagonal = frequency of each primitive
  • Off-diagonal = co-occurrence count
  • High values suggest primitives that "go together"
  • Lower triangle shown (matrix is symmetric)
Figure 7a: Recognition Network Training Curves
Training and validation loss over 30 epochs (left) and validation accuracy (right). The model converges smoothly with minimal overfitting.

📖 How to interpret this figure

  • Left panel: Training (blue) and validation (red) loss decreasing over epochs
  • Right panel: Validation accuracy increasing and stabilizing
  • The small gap between training and validation suggests good generalization
  • Final accuracy of 93.47% achieved after 30 epochs
Figure 7b: Per-Rule Recognition Network Accuracy
Accuracy distribution across all 57 rules, sorted from lowest to highest. Colors indicate relative performance (red = lower, green = higher).

📖 How to interpret this figure

  • Each bar represents one rule's recognition accuracy
  • Dashed blue line shows overall average (93.47%)
  • Most rules achieve 90%+ accuracy
  • Rules with complex scoring or unusual patterns may have lower accuracy

🌲 Shared Compositional Subtrees

What are shared subtrees?
When we represent each rule as a tree of function compositions, some subtrees appear in multiple rules. These shared subtrees are candidates for abstraction — reusable "chunks" that could be given names and added to our primitive library. Rules sharing subtrees are predicted to benefit from transfer learning.
Figure 7: Most Frequently Shared Compositional Subtrees
Each bar shows a subtree pattern that appears in multiple rules. Longer bars indicate subtrees shared by more rules, suggesting higher potential for transfer learning.

📖 How to interpret this figure

  • Each bar represents a specific composition pattern (shown as function notation)
  • Bar length = number of rules containing this exact subtree
  • Subtrees like arrays_equal(left_half, right_half) appear in many halves-comparison rules
  • These patterns are what DreamCoder's library learning would discover and name

Top 10 Shared Subtrees and Their Rules

Subtree Pattern Count Rules Using This Subtree
get_suit(first) 6 Half_map_samepos_M1, Half_map_samepos_M2, Step2_back_map_M1, Step2_back_map_M2, Adj_same_or_map_M1, +1 more
get_suit(second) 6 Half_map_samepos_M1, Half_map_samepos_M2, Step2_back_map_M1, Step2_back_map_M2, Adj_same_or_map_M1, +1 more
shifted_pairs(k=n/2) 4 Shift_half_plus_two, Shift_half_ge, Half_map_samepos_M1, Half_map_samepos_M2
shifted_pairs(k=2) 4 Shift2_plus3, Step2_back_map_M1, Step2_back_map_M2, Skip2_same_rank_or_suit
eq(get_suit) 4 Adj_same_or_map_M1, Adj_same_or_map_M2, Adj_same_rank_or_suit, Skip2_same_rank_or_suit
diff(get_rank_val) 3 Shift_half_plus_two, Shift2_plus3, Adj_rank_gap_le3
eq(cycle_M1(get_suit(first)), get_suit(second)) 3 Half_map_samepos_M1, Step2_back_map_M1, Adj_same_or_map_M1
cycle_M1(get_suit(first)) 3 Half_map_samepos_M1, Step2_back_map_M1, Adj_same_or_map_M1
eq(cycle_M2(get_suit(first)), get_suit(second)) 3 Half_map_samepos_M2, Step2_back_map_M2, Adj_same_or_map_M2
cycle_M2(get_suit(first)) 3 Half_map_samepos_M2, Step2_back_map_M2, Adj_same_or_map_M2
Why this matters for transfer learning:
If a participant learns a rule like "Halves_copy_suits", they acquire the subtree arrays_equal(map(get_suit, left_half), map(get_suit, right_half)). When they encounter "Halves_copy_colors", they can reuse most of this structure — only get_suit changes to get_color. This predicts faster learning!

How to read this catalogue:
Each rule shows its compositional decomposition using our primitive vocabulary. The Composition column shows the function composition tree. The Lambda notation shows the rule as a lambda expression. Primitives are the atomic building blocks used.

🌳 Lambda Composition Trees

Visual Rule Decomposition:
Each rule is built from primitive functions composed in a tree structure. The tree shows exactly how primitives combine to implement the rule's logic. A separate detailed composition trees page shows all 57 rules with both ASCII and graphical representations.

Sample Composition Trees

Here are a few representative examples showing how rules decompose into primitives:

Sorted_by_rank (r1x) Lambda: λh. ... ──────────────────────────────────────── Rule: Sorted_by_rank (r1x) Lambda: λh. ... ──────────────────────────────────────── is_sorted (strict=False) └── map └── get_rank_val
AP_len3_anywhere_anyk (r5x) Lambda: λh. ... ──────────────────────────────────────── Rule: AP_len3_anywhere_anyk (r5x) Lambda: λh. ... ──────────────────────────────────────── has_AP (length=3, step=any, aligned=False)
Suits_palindrome (r18x) Lambda: λh. ... ──────────────────────────────────────── Rule: Suits_palindrome (r18x) Lambda: λh. ... ──────────────────────────────────────── seq_palindrome └── get_suit
Halves_copy_colors (r22x) Lambda: λh. ... ──────────────────────────────────────── Rule: Halves_copy_colors (r22x) Lambda: λh. ... ──────────────────────────────────────── halves_equal └── map └── get_color

📄 Open full composition trees page →

🔍 Discovered Abstractions

What are abstractions?
Abstractions are compositional patterns that appear in multiple rules. In DreamCoder, these would be "learned" by the library learning component and added to the primitive vocabulary. Rules sharing abstractions are predicted to show transfer learning.

🧩 Shifted Pairs Check

Description: Check if all pairs at offset k satisfy a relation

λk. λR. λh. all(R, shifted_pairs(k, h))

Level: Level 2 Frequency: 8 rules

Used by: Shift_half_plus_two, Shift2_plus3, Shift_half_ge, Half_map_samepos_M1, Half_map_samepos_M2, Step2_back_map_M1...

🧩 Halves Property Equal (Boolean)

Description: Check if a boolean property holds equally in both halves (both true or both false)

λP. λh. P(left_half(h)) = P(right_half(h))

Level: Level 4 Frequency: 6 rules

Used by: Halves_uniform_color_equal, Halves_uniform_parity_equal, Halves_AP_step1_equal, Halves_hearts_presence_equal, Halves_AP_len3_any_equal, Halves_AP_len2_step1_equal

🧩 Halves Equal (Property)

Description: Check if applying a property function to both halves gives equal results

λF. λh. F(left_half(h)) = F(right_half(h))

Level: Level 4 Frequency: 5 rules

Used by: Halves_copy_suits, Halves_copy_colors, Halves_copy_ranks, Halves_copy_altcolor1, Halves_copy_altcolor2

🧩 Sequence Palindrome

Description: Check if the sequence of property values is a palindrome

λF. λh. map(F, h) = reverse(map(F, h))

Level: Level 4 Frequency: 5 rules

Used by: Suits_palindrome, Colors_palindrome, Ranks_palindrome, AltColor1_palindrome, AltColor2_palindrome

🧩 Adjacent Pairs Check

Description: Check if all adjacent pairs satisfy a relation

λR. λh. all(R, adjacent_pairs(h))

Level: Level 2 Frequency: 4 rules

Used by: Adj_same_rank_or_suit, Adj_rank_gap_le3, Adj_same_or_map_M1, Adj_same_or_map_M2

🧩 Terminals Equal

Description: Check if first and last cards share a property

λF. λh. F(first(h)) = F(last(h))

Level: Level 4 Frequency: 3 rules

Used by: Ends_same_suit, Ends_same_color, Ends_same_altcolor1

🧩 Bracket Match (PDA)

Description: Check if sequence forms well-matched brackets

λopeners. λclosers. λh. PDA_accept(h, openers, closers)

Level: Level 3 Frequency: 3 rules

Used by: Well_formed_brackets_by_suit, Even_opens_next_closes, Odd_opens_next_closes

🧩 Has Arithmetic Progression

Description: Check if hand contains an arithmetic progression of ranks

λlen. λstep. λaligned. λh. exists_AP(h, len, step, aligned)

Level: Level 3 Frequency: 3 rules

Used by: AP_len3_anywhere_anyk, AP_len3_step2_anywhere, AP_len4_step2_anywhere

🧩 Uniform Property

Description: Check if all cards share the same property value

λF. λh. unique_count(map(F, h)) = 1

Level: Level 1 Frequency: 2 rules

Used by: Uniform_color, Uniform_rank_parity

✅ Real Training Results:
  • Base Rate: How often random hands satisfy the rule (0-1)
  • Difficulty: Estimated learning difficulty (0=easy, 1=hard)
  • Model Accuracy: Recognition network's REAL accuracy from training (93.47% overall)

📖 Glossary & Interpretation Guide

Key Terms

Primitive
A basic function that cannot be decomposed further (e.g., get_suit, first)
Combinator
A function that combines other functions (e.g., map, filter, all)
Composition
The tree structure showing how a rule is built from primitives
Abstraction
A reusable pattern discovered across multiple rules
Level
The compositional complexity (0=atomic, 4=meta-combinator)
Base Rate
The probability that a random hand satisfies the rule
Recognition Network
Neural network that predicts which primitives are needed for a task

How to Interpret Results

  1. Transfer Prediction: Rules sharing abstractions (see Section 4) should show transfer. For example, learning Halves_copy_suits should help with Halves_copy_colors because both use halves_equal.
  2. Difficulty Prediction: Higher-level rules with more primitives should be harder to learn. Check the performance table (Section 5) to see if this holds.
  3. Curriculum Design: Start with Level 0-1 rules, then progress to higher levels. Within a family, order by base rate (extreme base rates first).

Generated by DreamCoder Card Game Modeling System

Based on Ellis et al. (2023) - DreamCoder: Growing Generalizable, Interpretable Knowledge with Wake-Sleep Bayesian Program Learning
